skill.name: firebase-auth-basics
skill.description: Guides setup and use of Firebase Authentication for web apps, including sign-in providers, auth state, email links, and auth-aware security rules.
skill.summary: Set up Firebase Authentication and protect Firebase data with auth-aware rules.
skill.author: firebase

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported many command execution, weak cryptography, and network patterns, but review found these are markdown examples, Firebase CLI references, localhost emulator setup, or documentation links. No prompt injection, malicious code execution, credential exfiltration, or hidden behavior was found. The main confirmed concern is an email-link sample that stores an email address in browser localStorage.

medium_findings.title: Email Address Stored In Browser LocalStorage
medium_findings.description: The email-link authentication example stores a user's email address in window.localStorage, reads it later, and removes it after successful sign-in. This is a privacy-sensitive browser storage pattern because localStorage is persistent and accessible to scripts running in the origin.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The storage operations are explicit and tied to an email address used during sign-in. The pattern is common in Firebase examples, but it still creates a browser privacy risk if copied without safeguards.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Findings Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The command execution findings point to markdown code fences, inline Firebase CLI examples, imports, and Firebase rules syntax. I found no skill script, shell execution logic, Ruby backtick execution, or user-controlled command execution path.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are markdown documentation and code samples, not executable marketplace code. The only actual external command guidance is a Firebase CLI command that users may choose to run.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are Authentication Terminology False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography findings align with authentication terms, token handling, and email-link action settings, not cryptographic algorithm choices. No MD5, SHA-1, custom crypto implementation, or insecure cipher usage was found in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines describe Firebase Authentication APIs and an action-code settings object. There is no evidence of cryptographic primitive selection or implementation.

low_findings.title: Network Findings Are Expected Firebase Documentation Patterns
low_findings.description: The network locations reference a localhost Auth emulator, an example return URL, Firebase API documentation, and Firebase Console. These URLs support normal Firebase setup and do not send secrets to an unknown endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are either local development endpoints, placeholder examples, or Firebase-owned documentation and console pages. I found no hidden network call or exfiltration behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Persistent Browser Storage For Sign-In Email
dangerous_patterns.description: The email-link workflow persists an email address in localStorage before sign-in completes. Users should prefer short-lived storage, clear the value on failure paths, and consider XSS exposure when copying the example.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is directly shown in the code sample. It is not malicious, but persistent browser storage is a recognized risk for personal data.

content.value_statement: Adding sign-in to a Firebase app requires provider setup, client code, and secure data rules. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical Firebase Auth guidance for common web workflows.
actual_capabilities: Explains Firebase Authentication concepts, users, providers, ID tokens, and refresh tokens.
Guides enabling email, anonymous, and Google sign-in through Firebase CLI configuration.
Provides web SDK examples for provider sign-in, anonymous sign-in, email links, auth state, and sign-out.
Shows how to connect the web app to the Firebase Auth emulator during local development.
Introduces auth-aware Firestore and Storage rules using request.auth and token claims.
limitations: It focuses on web client examples and does not cover native mobile SDK setup.
It does not replace detailed provider configuration in the Firebase Console.
It gives basic security rule examples, not a full rules audit for a production app.
It does not implement backend token verification or custom authentication servers.

use_cases.title: Add User Sign-In To A Web App
use_cases.description: Use the skill to choose a Firebase Auth provider and add the matching web SDK flow.

use_cases.title: Protect Firebase Data By User Identity
use_cases.description: Use request.auth examples to draft rules that require sign-in or ownership checks.

use_cases.title: Prototype Auth With Local Emulators
use_cases.description: Connect a local web app to the Auth emulator before using production Firebase resources.

prompt_templates.title: Start Firebase Auth
prompt_templates.prompt: Use firebase-auth-basics to explain the Firebase Auth setup steps for my web app. Include providers, client setup, and the first security rule checks.

prompt_templates.title: Add Provider Sign-In
prompt_templates.prompt: Use firebase-auth-basics to help me add Google sign-in and email/password sign-in to an existing Firebase web app. Mention any Firebase Console steps.

prompt_templates.title: Review Auth Rules
prompt_templates.prompt: Use firebase-auth-basics to review my Firestore or Storage rules for request.auth usage, ownership checks, and email verification checks.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Multi-Provider Auth
prompt_templates.prompt: Use firebase-auth-basics to plan a Firebase Auth flow with anonymous users, account linking, OAuth providers, auth state handling, and secure data access.

output_examples.input: I need Google sign-in for a Firebase web app.
output_examples.output: A concise setup plan covering Firebase Console provider enablement, web SDK initialization, a Google provider flow, error handling, and auth state handling.

output_examples.input: How should I protect user profile documents?
output_examples.output: A rule design that requires a signed-in user and checks that the document path or owner field matches the authenticated user ID.

output_examples.input: I want passwordless sign-in with email links.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step flow for sending the link, completing sign-in on the landing page, and handling email confirmation storage carefully.

best_practices: Enable only the sign-in providers your app actually needs.
Use request.auth and ownership checks before granting user data access.
Test authentication flows with the emulator before using production resources.
anti_patterns: Do not rely on client-side checks as the only protection for Firebase data.
Do not store tokens or personal data in persistent browser storage without a clear need.
Do not enable broad read or write rules just to make sign-in work faster.

faq.question: Does this skill create Firebase projects?
faq.answer: No. It references Firebase CLI setup but mainly guides authentication configuration and client usage.

faq.question: Which platforms does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers web SDK examples. It does not cover iOS, Android, Flutter, Unity, or C++ setup.

faq.question: Can it help with Google sign-in?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes Google provider guidance and explains that Google sign-in is a secure default provider.

faq.question: Does it include security rules?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes basic request.auth examples for signed-in users, ownership checks, and email verification checks.

faq.question: Is it safe for production apps?
faq.answer: Use it as a starting point. Production apps still need provider review, rules testing, and privacy review.

faq.question: Can Claude Code or Codex use it?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill is compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
